docs.title: AI Data Ingestion Engine (AIDE) — Architecture & Engineering Handbook
docs.text:
Project Codename: AI Data Ingestion Engine (AIDE), also referred to as the GrowEasy Smart Import Engine.

This handbook documents the complete architecture of an AI-powered data-ingestion platform. Its first client application is an AI-powered CSV Importer that extracts CRM lead information from any valid CSV format — Facebook Lead exports, Google Ads exports, real-estate CRM exports, sales reports, agency spreadsheets — and maps them into a canonical CRM schema using large language models.

The core engineering challenge is not parsing CSV files. It is accepting files with unknown column names, layouts, and structures, and still extracting the required CRM fields accurately, safely, and at scale.

docs.title: How the System Works
docs.text:
At the highest level, the platform is a staged processing pipeline in which every stage has a well-defined contract (see Chapter 4 — The Pipeline Architecture Mindset):

flowchart TD
    A[Upload] --> B[File Validation Layer]
    B --> C[Streaming CSV Parser]
    C --> D[Data Normalization Engine]
    D --> E[AI Extraction Engine]
    E --> F[Validation & Trust Engine]
    F --> G[CRM Mapping & Batch Execution]
    G --> H[Structured CRM Records]
    H --> I[Result Presentation & Reporting]

Around this pipeline sit the production concerns that turn an application into a platform: observability, reliability, security, quality engineering, and platform/DevOps engineering (Chapters 15–19).

docs.title: How to Read This Book
docs.text: The book is organized into six parts. Each design chapter (5–19) corresponds to one engineering volume — a self-contained unit of design that could be implemented independently and merged into production — and ends with an Implementation Tasks checklist.

docs.title: Design Principles
docs.text:
Three principles recur throughout the book:

Applications solve business problems. Platforms enable applications to evolve safely.

Think in terms of a processing pipeline where each stage has a well-defined contract — not in terms of Frontend ↔ Backend ↔ AI. That makes the system easier to test, easier to debug, and much easier to extend.

AI output is a proposal, not a fact. Every model response passes through validation and trust scoring before it becomes a CRM record.

docs.title: 1. Context
docs.text:
This handbook documents the design and engineering of an AI-powered CSV Importer built for the GrowEasy Software Developer assignment (Intern / Full-Time track).

Item Detail Company GrowEasy — https://groweasy.ai Work mode Work From Home (WFH) Joining Immediate Internship compensation ₹15,000 – ₹20,000 per month Full-time compensation ₹35,000 – ₹50,000 per month Full-time prerequisite Minimum 1 year of full-time software development experience Submission deadline 12 July 2026

docs.title: 2. Objective
docs.text:
Build an AI-powered CSV Importer that can intelligently extract CRM lead information from any valid CSV format.

The challenge is not parsing CSV files. The challenge is allowing users to upload CSVs with different column names, layouts, and structures, while the system accurately maps and extracts the required CRM fields using AI.

The application must identify the appropriate fields in an arbitrary CSV and convert them into GrowEasy CRM format.

docs.title: 2.1 Example Input Variations
docs.text:
All of the following CSV origins must work:

  • Facebook Lead Export
  • Google Ads Export
  • Excel sheets
  • Real Estate CRM exports
  • Sales reports
  • Marketing agency CSVs
  • Manually created spreadsheets

docs.title: 3. Functional Requirements
docs.text: The project consists of both a Frontend and a Backend.

docs.title: 3.1 Frontend Requirements
docs.text:
Create a responsive web application implementing the following four-step workflow.

flowchart LR
    A["Step 1 — Upload CSV"] --> B["Step 2 — Preview (no AI yet)"]
    B --> C["Step 3 — Confirm Import"]
    C --> D["Step 4 — Display Parsed Result"]

docs.title: Step 1 — Upload CSV
docs.text:
Allow users to upload a valid CSV file. Examples:

  • Drag & Drop upload
  • File Picker

docs.title: Step 2 — Preview
docs.text:
After upload:

  • Parse the CSV
  • Show a preview of uploaded rows
  • Display data inside a beautiful responsive table

The table should support:

  • Horizontal scrolling
  • Vertical scrolling
  • Sticky headers (preferred)
  • Responsive design

No AI processing should happen at this stage.

docs.title: Step 3 — Confirm Import
docs.text: Provide a Confirm button. Only after confirmation should the frontend call the backend API.

docs.title: Step 4 — Display Parsed Result
docs.text:
The backend returns AI-extracted CRM records. Display the parsed result in another responsive table, showing:

  • Successfully parsed records
  • Skipped records (if any)
  • Total imported
  • Total skipped

docs.title: 3.2 Backend Requirements
docs.text:
Create APIs that:

  1. Accept CSV Upload. Accept any valid CSV file. Do not assume column names are fixed.
  2. Parse CSV. Convert the CSV into records.
  3. AI Extraction. Send records to an AI model in batches. The AI should intelligently map available fields into GrowEasy CRM format. OpenAI, Gemini, Claude, or any equivalent LLM may be used.
  4. Return Structured JSON. Return extracted CRM records in JSON format.

docs.title: 4. Required CRM Fields
docs.text:
The AI should extract as many of the following fields as possible.

Field Description created_at Lead creation date name Lead name email Primary email country_code Country code mobile_without_country_code Mobile number company Company name city City state State country Country lead_owner Lead owner crm_status Lead status crm_note Notes/remarks data_source Source possession_time Property possession time description Additional description

created_at,name,email,country_code,mobile_without_country_code,company,city,state,country,lead_owner,crm_status,crm_note,data_source,possession_time,description

docs.title: 5. AI Extraction Rules
docs.text: The AI must follow these rules while extracting records.

docs.title: 5.1 Allowed CRM Status Values
docs.text:
crm_status may only be one of:

  • GOOD_LEAD_FOLLOW_UP
  • DID_NOT_CONNECT
  • BAD_LEAD
  • SALE_DONE

docs.title: 5.2 Allowed Data Source Values
docs.text:
data_source may only be one of:

  • leads_on_demand
  • meridian_tower
  • eden_park
  • varah_swamy
  • sarjapur_plots

If none match confidently, leave it blank.

docs.title: 5.3 Date Format
docs.text:
created_at must be convertible using JavaScript:

new Date(created_at)

docs.title: 5.4 CRM Notes
docs.text:
Use crm_note for:

  • Remarks
  • Follow-up notes
  • Additional comments
  • Extra phone numbers
  • Extra email addresses
  • Any useful information that doesn't fit another field

docs.title: 5.5 Multiple Emails or Mobile Numbers
docs.text:
If multiple email addresses exist:

  • Use the first email.
  • Append remaining emails into crm_note.

If multiple mobile numbers exist:

  • Use the first mobile.
  • Append remaining numbers into crm_note.

docs.title: 5.6 CSV Compatibility
docs.text: Each record must remain a single CSV row. Avoid introducing unintended line breaks. If line breaks are necessary, escape them appropriately (for example, \n) so the CSV remains valid.

docs.title: 5.7 Skip Invalid Records
docs.text: If a record contains neither an email nor a mobile number, skip that record.

Additional credit is given for implementing:

docs.title: 1. Reading the Assignment Correctly
docs.text:
The assignment in Chapter 1 — Assignment Specification is not a test of CSV parsing. It tests whether the builder thinks like an engineer building an AI product. Viewed through a senior reviewer's lens, submissions are weighted roughly as follows:

Area Weight AI Pipeline ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Backend Architecture ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Engineering Decisions ⭐⭐⭐⭐ UI/UX ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Code Quality ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Deployment ⭐⭐⭐

The task should therefore not be framed as:

"Build a CSV importer."

but as:

"Build a production AI data ingestion pipeline."

docs.title: 2. The Real Problem: Schema-Free Semantic Mapping
docs.text:
GrowEasy receives CSVs from thousands of companies, and every company exports CRM data differently.

Example 1:

Customer Name
Email Address
Phone

Example 2:

Lead
Mail ID
Contact Number

Example 3:

Owner Name
Primary Contact
Cell

Example 4:

Lead Details
Customer Info

There is no fixed schema. The AI must understand that Mail ID means email and Customer Name means name — without any predefined mapping. That is the real challenge.

docs.title: 3. Naive Pipeline vs Engineered Pipeline
docs.text:
The junior-level solution is:

Frontend → POST CSV → OpenAI → Done

The engineered solution treats the system as a real SaaS product, where the AI call is only one small stage of a larger pipeline:

flowchart TD
    A[Upload CSV] --> B[CSV Validation Layer]
    B --> C[Streaming CSV Parser]
    C --> D[Row Normalization Engine]
    D --> E["Batch Builder (50 rows)"]
    E --> F[AI Extraction Pipeline]
    F --> G[JSON Schema Validation]
    G --> H[Record Post Processing]
    H --> I["Duplicate Detection (optional)"]
    I --> J[Statistics Generator]
    J --> K[API Response]

Notice how AI is only one small component of the whole.

docs.title: 4. Backend Module Architecture
docs.text:
The backend is separated into independent modules, each with exactly one responsibility:

API

services
    csv/
    ai/
    validation/
    crm/
    batching/
    statistics/
    logging/
    prompt/
    utils/

This layering is elaborated in Chapter 7 — Backend Architecture.

docs.title: 5. Deterministic Preprocessing Before AI
docs.text:
A common mistake is sending raw CSV content straight to the AI. AI tokens are expensive; the data must be cleaned first. The cleaner the data before AI, the better the AI performs.

Normalization examples:

Input Normalized Phone column value 9876543210 9876543210 +91-98765 43210 +919876543210 john @gmail.com john@gmail.com 2026/05/12 ISO date

The preprocessing chain runs as a fixed sequence, and only then is the AI invoked:

CSV
 → Trim spaces
 → Normalize encoding
 → Remove empty columns
 → Remove duplicate headers
 → Normalize dates
 → Normalize phones
 → Normalize emails
 → Detect blank rows
 → Create clean records

This becomes the Data Normalization Engine (Chapter 9).

docs.title: 6. AI Performs Semantic Mapping, Not CSV Parsing
docs.text:
There is a huge difference between asking the AI to parse a CSV and asking it to perform semantic mapping on already-structured records.

The backend gives the AI a clean record:

{
  "Customer Name": "John",
  "Contact": "9876543210",
  "Mail": "abc@gmail.com",
  "Remarks": "Interested"
}

The AI only answers with the mapped CRM record:

{
  "name": "John",
  "mobile": "9876543210",
  "email": "abc@gmail.com",
  "crm_note": "Interested"
}

This is far more reliable than handing the model raw CSV text.

docs.title: 7. Prompt Design Philosophy
docs.text:
The prompt is arguably 40% of the assignment. A prompt of the form "Extract CRM fields." is insufficient. Instead, the prompt is designed as a contract:

You are an enterprise CRM ingestion engine.

Your job is NOT to summarize.
Your job is NOT to infer imaginary values.

Only map information that exists.
Never hallucinate.
If confidence is low return null.
Return valid JSON only.

Allowed CRM status:
...

Allowed data source:
...

Rules:
...

Examples:
...

Counter Examples:
...

Output schema:
...

Principle: Prompt quality matters more than model choice.

Full prompt design is covered in Chapter 11 — Prompt Engineering & Semantic Intelligence.

docs.title: 8. Batch Processing
docs.text:
Never send 5,000 rows to the model in a single request. Instead, the CSV is split into chunks (for example, 25 rows each) which are processed in parallel:

CSV → chunks → [25 rows] [25 rows] [25 rows] … → parallel AI requests

Production systems batch requests to balance latency, throughput, and cost. Parallelism must respect provider rate limits.

docs.title: 9. JSON Validation — Never Trust AI
docs.text:
This is where many projects fail. Every AI response is validated (for example, with Zod) before it is accepted:

flowchart TD
    A[AI returns JSON] --> B{Zod schema valid?}
    B -->|yes| C[Continue pipeline]
    B -->|no| D[Retry]

Validation happens always, on every response.

docs.title: 10. Retry Strategy
docs.text:
Suppose the model returns broken JSON:

{
"name":

The production response is a layered recovery ladder — never crash the entire import:

Attempt 1
 → failed
 → repair JSON
 → failed
 → retry
 → failed
 → mark batch failed
 → continue next batch

A failed batch is isolated; the remaining batches still complete.

docs.title: 11. Confidence Checks
docs.text:
Instead of blindly accepting AI output, each record passes through an acceptance checklist:

AI output
 → validator
 → required field present?
 → email valid?
 → phone valid?
 → status allowed?
 → date valid?
 → accept

docs.title: 12. Skip-Rule Engine
docs.text:
The assignment mandates skipping records with no email and no phone. Rather than an inline if, this is a dedicated rule engine that produces a reason for every skip:

Rule Engine
 → Missing email?
 → Missing phone?
 → Skip
 → Reason: "No contact information"

The UI can then show each skipped record with its reason ("Skipped — Reason: No contact info"), which is a much better user experience. See Chapter 13 — Validation, Business Rules & Trust Engine.

docs.title: 13. Frontend Flow
docs.text:
Rather than the minimal Upload → Preview → Import flow, the frontend is designed as a complete ingestion journey:

flowchart TD
    A[Upload] --> B[Validation]
    B --> C[Preview]
    C --> D[File statistics]
    D --> E[Confirm]
    E --> F[Progress]
    F --> G[Live processing]
    G --> H[Results]
    H --> I[Download JSON]
    I --> J[Done]

docs.title: 13.1 During Preview
docs.text:
The preview screen shows file intelligence, not just rows:

Rows
Columns
Missing Values
Duplicate Headers
Encoding
Estimated Tokens
Estimated AI Cost
Detected Delimiter

docs.title: 13.2 During Import
docs.text:
Instead of a spinner, show live pipeline progress:

███████░░░░

Rows processed   350 / 1200
Current batch    8 / 24
AI requests      7 complete
Skipped          23
Imported         327

Even if internally this is driven by batch-completion updates, it reads as a real ingestion pipeline.

docs.title: 13.3 Result Screen
docs.text:
The result is a dashboard, not only a table:

Imported          421
Skipped           17
Success           96%
Detected Fields   14
Unknown Columns   6
Duration          18 sec

docs.title: 14. Error Handling Catalogue
docs.text:
The system must handle, at minimum:

Invalid CSV
Huge file
UTF-16
Duplicate headers
Missing delimiter
Corrupted encoding
Empty file
Malformed quotes
GPT timeout
GPT rate limit
JSON parse failure
AI hallucination
Network failure
Batch retry exhausted

docs.title: 15. Logging
docs.text:
Production applications log every stage of the import lifecycle:

Import Started
 → CSV Parsed
 → Batch Created
 → Batch 1 Sent
 → AI Returned
 → Validation Passed
 → Statistics Updated
 → Completed

Structured logs make debugging and observability much easier (see Chapter 15 — Observability).

docs.text:
Next.js  → Vercel
Backend  → Railway
AI       → OpenAI
GitHub   → Actions → Auto Deploy

docs.title: 17. Repository Layout
docs.text:
apps/
  web/
  api/

packages/
  shared-types/
  validation/
  prompt/
  ui/

infra/
  docker/

Even for a small project, separating reusable pieces demonstrates architectural thinking.

docs.title: 18. Differentiating Features
docs.text:
Beyond the base requirements, the following features materially strengthen the solution:

  1. Schema inference before AI. Detect obvious fields (email, phone, date) with deterministic logic and only ask the LLM to resolve ambiguous columns. This reduces cost and improves consistency.
  2. Streaming CSV parsing so large files don't consume excessive memory.
  3. Prompt versioning so prompt changes are tracked independently.
  4. JSON schema validation with automatic retry on malformed responses.
  5. Observability with request IDs, structured logs, and timing metrics.
  6. Large-file support using virtualized tables on the frontend.
  7. Dockerized local development with one command to start everything.
  8. Unit tests for preprocessing, validation, and rule engine logic.
  9. Rate limiting and concurrency control for AI requests.
  10. Download results as CSV or JSON after processing.

docs.title: 19. Design Recommendation
docs.text:
The system is built as if it were the first module of a commercial AI CRM ingestion platform. That means emphasizing:

  • Clean, layered architecture (separation of concerns)
  • AI as one component, not the whole application
  • Deterministic preprocessing and validation before and after the LLM
  • Fault tolerance (batching, retries, graceful failures)
  • Excellent user experience (preview, progress, summaries, clear errors)
  • Production engineering practices (logging, testing, deployment, documentation)

This approach demonstrates far more than framework knowledge — it shows how to design reliable AI-powered systems that can evolve beyond a coding assignment.

docs.title: Implementation Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] Task 2.1 — Schema inference pre-pass. Detect obvious fields (email, phone, date) deterministically and send only ambiguous columns to the LLM.
  • [ ] Task 2.2 — Streaming CSV parser. Parse large files as streams so memory usage stays bounded.
  • [ ] Task 2.3 — Prompt versioning. Track prompt changes independently of application code.
  • [ ] Task 2.4 — JSON schema validation with retry. Validate every AI response against a schema and automatically retry malformed responses.
  • [ ] Task 2.5 — Observability baseline. Add request IDs, structured logs, and timing metrics across the pipeline.
  • [ ] Task 2.6 — Virtualized preview tables. Support large files on the frontend with virtualized rendering.
  • [ ] Task 2.7 — Dockerized development. Provide one-command local startup for the full stack.
  • [ ] Task 2.8 — Unit tests for deterministic layers. Cover preprocessing, validation, and rule-engine logic with unit tests.
  • [ ] Task 2.9 — Rate limiting and concurrency control. Enforce provider-aware limits on parallel AI requests.
  • [ ] Task 2.10 — Result export. Allow downloading processed results as CSV or JSON.

docs.title: 1. Methodology: Engineering Volumes
docs.text:
The system is designed and documented not as loose notes but as engineering volumes. Each volume ends with something that could be implemented independently and merged into production. A volume is equivalent to what a senior engineer would design before writing code.

The documentation standard for the full roadmap:

  • 18 volumes
  • Around 250–400 pages worth of engineering documentation when complete
  • Every design decision justified
  • Architecture diagrams, sequence diagrams, folder structures, and production considerations
  • Written so the system can be both implemented and defended confidently in a technical discussion

The roadmap starts with Volume 1 — Product Thinking & System Architecture, because every later decision (frontend, backend, AI pipeline, deployment, testing) naturally builds on that foundation.

docs.text:
The project is deliberately not called "CSV Importer". It is built like a real product:

Project Codename: AI Data Ingestion Engine (AIDE) — also referred to as the GrowEasy Smart Import Engine.

The assignment becomes merely the first client of this engine.

Plus: animations, loading states, skeletons, virtualized tables, progress bars, notifications.

docs.title: Volume 12 — Error Recovery System
docs.text:
Production systems fail. Scope:

  • malformed CSV
  • timeout
  • invalid AI response
  • retry
  • rollback
  • partial imports
  • recoverability

docs.title: Volume 13 — Observability
docs.text: Professional engineers build observable systems: logging, metrics, tracing, import analytics, performance dashboard, request IDs, timing.

docs.title: Volume 14 — Security
docs.text: Scope: CSV injection, DoS, prompt injection, file limits, rate limits, validation, secrets, authentication (future).

docs.title: Volume 15 — Testing Strategy
docs.text: Scope: unit, integration, contract, AI prompt tests, CSV fixtures, mocking, regression testing.

docs.title: Volume 16 — Deployment
docs.text: Scope: Docker, Railway, Vercel, environment variables, CI/CD, GitHub Actions, production build.

docs.title: Volume 17 — README & Documentation
docs.text: Interview-quality README covering: architecture, flow, setup, screenshots, API, folder structure, future scope.

docs.title: Volume 18 — Future Evolution
docs.text:
How the system becomes an AI Data Platform instead of a CSV importer. Future ideas:

  • Excel
  • PDF
  • OCR
  • Images
  • APIs
  • CRM Connectors: Salesforce, HubSpot, Zoho
  • Incremental Imports
  • Human-in-the-loop review
  • Fine-tuned extraction models

docs.title: 4. Target Architecture
docs.text:
Most naive solutions amount to:

CSV → GPT → JSON

This roadmap instead designs the architecture of a production-grade ingestion platform:

flowchart TD
    A[Upload] --> B[File Validation Layer]
    B --> C[Streaming CSV Processing Engine]
    C --> D[Data Normalization Engine]
    D --> E[Intelligent Batch Builder]
    E --> F[AI Extraction Pipeline]
    F --> G[Response Validation Layer]
    G --> H[Business Rule Engine]
    H --> I[CRM Transformation Engine]
    I --> J[Statistics & Analytics]
    J --> K[Final API Response]
    K --> L[Interactive Result UI]

docs.title: 5. Roadmap-to-Chapter Mapping
docs.text:

Roadmap Volume(s) Book Chapter(s) Notes Volume 1 — Product Thinking & System Architecture Chapter 5 Direct mapping Volume 2 — Frontend Architecture Chapter 6 Direct mapping Volume 3 — Backend Architecture Chapter 7 Direct mapping Volume 4 — CSV Processing Engine Chapter 8 Direct mapping Volume 5 — Data Normalization Engine Chapter 9 Direct mapping Volume 6 — AI Extraction Engine Chapter 10 Direct mapping Volume 7 — AI Prompt Engineering Masterclass Chapter 11 and Chapter 12 Expanded into two chapters Volume 8 — Validation Engine Chapter 13 Direct mapping Volumes 9–10 — CRM Mapping Engine, Batch Processing Engine Chapter 14 Consolidated into the Execution Engine Volume 12 — Error Recovery System Chapter 16 Evolved into Reliability & Resilience Volume 13 — Observability Chapter 15 Evolved into Observability & Telemetry Volume 14 — Security Chapter 17 Evolved into Security & AI Safety Volume 15 — Testing Strategy Chapter 18 Evolved into Quality Engineering Volume 16 — Deployment Chapter 19 Evolved into Platform Engineering & DevOps Volumes 17–18 — README & Documentation, Future Evolution Chapter 20 Covered by Future Evolution & Platform Vision

In short: roadmap Volumes 1–6 map to Chapters 5–10; Volume 7 was expanded into Chapters 11 and 12; Volume 8 became Chapter 13; Volumes 9–10 were consolidated into Chapter 14; Volumes 12–16 evolved into Chapters 15–19 (Observability, Reliability, Security, Quality, Platform Engineering); and Volumes 17–18 are covered by Chapter 20. Volume 11 (Frontend UX Engineering) is absorbed into the frontend chapters (6) and the result/progress UX described throughout.

docs.title: Chapter 4 — The Pipeline Architecture Mindset
docs.text:
The difference between a feature-level implementation and a systems-level design of this importer comes down to one architectural choice. A straightforward implementation treats the application as a chain of requests:

Frontend → Backend → OpenAI → Frontend

This is a request–response architecture. It works, but it does not scale well, because responsibilities become mixed across layers.

docs.title: The Pipeline Mindset
docs.text:
Instead, think of the application like a factory — a car manufacturing plant. The car is not built by one worker; it passes through multiple stations:

flowchart LR
    A[Raw Steel] --> B[Frame Assembly] --> C[Engine Installation] --> D[Painting] --> E[Testing] --> F[Quality Check] --> G[Packaging]

Every station has only one responsibility. If something goes wrong, you immediately know where.

The importer should work exactly the same way. Instead of

CSV → AI → JSON

we build a staged pipeline:

flowchart TD
    A[CSV] --> B[Validation] --> C[Parser] --> D[Normalizer] --> E[Batch Builder] --> F[AI Extraction] --> G[Validator] --> H[Business Rules] --> I[Statistics] --> J[Formatter] --> K[Frontend]

Notice something important: only one stage actually uses AI. Everything else is deterministic. That makes the system much more reliable.

docs.title: Every Stage Has a Contract
docs.text:
A contract means:

"This stage receives exactly this data and always returns exactly this data."

Think of contracts as APIs inside the application. The first stage, for example, accepts a raw CSV file and returns file metadata — nothing else:

{
  "fileName": "...",
  "size": "...",
  "delimiter": "...",
  "encoding": "..."
}

The full chain of contracts:

Stage Input Output Stage 1 — File Intake CSV File File Metadata (fileName, size, delimiter, encoding) Stage 2 — Parser File Metadata Parsed Records[] Stage 3 — Normalizer Parsed Records[] Normalized Records[] Stage 4 — Batch Builder Normalized Records[] Batches[] Stage 5 — AI Extraction Batch CRM Records

Every stage is completely independent.

docs.title: Why This Is Powerful
docs.text:
Suppose the AI suddenly starts returning bad JSON. Where is the bug? Not in the frontend. Not in the parser. Not in batching. Only in the AI Extraction Stage. You immediately know where to debug.

Suppose CSV parsing fails. Only the CSV Parser stage is implicated; nothing else changes.

Suppose the company later wants Excel support. In a request–response architecture, most implementations require a broad rewrite. In a pipeline architecture, you replace the CSV Parser with an Excel Parser — everything downstream stays exactly the same. That is why enterprise systems are built around pipelines.

docs.title: Think Like Unix
docs.text:
The Unix philosophy says:

One program should do one thing well.

The pipeline stages follow the same idea:

Module Sole Responsibility Parser Only parses Normalizer Only cleans Batch Builder Only creates batches AI Only extracts meaning Validator Only validates Statistics Only computes metrics

No module should perform two unrelated responsibilities.

docs.title: Why Testing Becomes Easy
docs.text:
Suppose we want to test normalization. Do we need OpenAI? No. Do we need the frontend? No. Do we need Express? No.

Input:

{ "phone": "+91 98765-43210" }

Expected output:

{ "phone": "+919876543210" }

Done. Testing becomes extremely simple because every stage is isolated.

docs.title: Why Future Features Become Easy
docs.text:
Imagine the CRM team later asks for PDF imports. In the old architecture (Frontend → Backend → AI), that is a huge rewrite. In the pipeline architecture:

PDF Parser → Normalizer → Batch Builder → AI → Validator → Done

Only one module changes. Everything else stays untouched.

docs.title: Error Isolation
docs.text:
Instead of a generic failure report such as Import Failed, the pipeline tells us exactly which stage failed and why:

Failing Stage Reason CSV Validation Unsupported Encoding AI Extraction Malformed JSON Business Rules Missing Contact Information

That makes debugging much easier.

docs.title: Production Systems Work This Way
docs.text:
Many large-scale systems follow this exact philosophy:

  • ETL pipelines: Extract → Transform → Load
  • CI/CD pipelines: Build → Test → Package → Deploy
  • Video processing: Upload → Transcode → Optimize → Distribute
  • Search indexing: Crawl → Parse → Tokenize → Rank → Index
  • ML pipelines: Ingest → Clean → Feature Engineer → Train → Evaluate

The AI importer is really another form of a data-processing pipeline.

docs.title: The Final Pipeline
docs.text:
This is the architecture recommended for the entire project:

flowchart TD
    A[File Upload Stage] --> B[File Validation Stage]
    B --> C[CSV Parsing Stage]
    C --> D[Data Normalization Stage]
    D --> E[Batch Creation Stage]
    E --> F[AI Extraction Stage]
    F --> G[Response Validation]
    G --> H[Business Rule Engine]
    H --> I[Statistics Generation]
    I --> J[Response Transformation]
    J --> K[Frontend Rendering]

Each stage has a single, explicit responsibility and a clear input/output contract. That architecture is easier to reason about, easier to test independently, and flexible enough to support new input formats or processing steps without rewriting the rest of the system. The frontend that orchestrates this workflow is designed in Chapter 6 — Frontend Architecture, and the backend implementation of each stage begins in Chapter 7 — Backend Architecture.

docs.text:
• Chapter 2 — Solution Analysis & Design Approach — the initial analysis that this pipeline philosophy refines
  • Chapter 5 — Product Thinking & System Architecture — the system architecture that instantiates this pipeline end to end
  • Chapter 7 — Backend Architecture — maps every backend module to a pipeline stage
  • Chapter 14 — Execution Engine, Orchestration & Concurrency — how the pipeline stages are executed and coordinated at runtime

docs.title: Chapter 5 — Product Thinking & System Architecture
docs.text:
Goal: Build a production-grade AI-powered data ingestion engine capable of intelligently extracting CRM lead information from heterogeneous CSV datasets while maintaining high accuracy, scalability, fault tolerance, and an exceptional user experience.

This chapter is written as an internal engineering design document — the kind a senior engineer would present before implementation.

docs.title: 1. Executive Summary
docs.text:
Modern businesses receive lead data from dozens of platforms:

  • Facebook Lead Ads
  • Google Ads
  • Real Estate CRMs
  • Marketing Agencies
  • Sales Teams
  • Excel Sheets
  • Internal CRMs
  • Manual spreadsheets

Every source exports data differently. Although the information is semantically similar, the structure varies significantly.

Example:

CSV A CSV B CSV C Name Customer Full Name Email Mail ID Contact Email Phone Mobile Primary Contact

Traditional CSV importers rely on predefined mappings or manual user intervention. Our objective is to eliminate manual mapping by leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) to understand the semantic meaning of columns and automatically transform them into GrowEasy CRM's canonical schema.

The application must behave like a production-grade ingestion platform rather than a simple CSV parser.

docs.title: Current Industry Problem
docs.text:
Organizations exchange customer data through spreadsheets every day. Unfortunately:

  • Every company defines different column names.
  • Data quality varies significantly.
  • Some exports contain unnecessary columns.
  • Important fields are mixed with notes.
  • Contact information appears in inconsistent formats.
  • Multiple emails and phone numbers may exist.
  • Missing values are common.

A traditional importer cannot reliably handle these variations. Users spend considerable time manually mapping columns before importing data.

docs.title: Proposed Solution
docs.text:
Develop an AI-powered ingestion engine that:

  • Accepts arbitrary CSV formats.
  • Understands the semantic meaning of each column.
  • Extracts CRM-compatible information.
  • Validates the extracted data.
  • Skips unusable records.
  • Returns a clean structured dataset ready for CRM ingestion.

The user should never need to manually map columns.

docs.title: 3. Product Vision
docs.text:
Instead of building

CSV → JSON Converter

we are building

An Intelligent Data Understanding Platform

CSV is only the first supported input format. The architecture should be extensible enough to support:

  • Excel
  • JSON
  • APIs
  • PDFs
  • OCR Documents
  • Images
  • CRM Connectors

without redesigning the core system. (See Chapter 20 — Future Evolution & Platform Vision for the long-term platform direction.)

docs.title: 4. Core Objectives
docs.text: The system should satisfy the following objectives.

docs.title: Functional Objectives
docs.text:
• Accept any valid CSV.
  • Parse efficiently.
  • Detect column semantics.
  • Extract CRM fields.
  • Skip invalid records.
  • Produce structured output.
  • Display import statistics.
  • Support thousands of rows.

docs.title: Engineering Objectives
docs.text:
• Modular architecture
  • Clean separation of concerns
  • AI isolation
  • Testability
  • Scalability
  • Production readiness

docs.title: User Experience Objectives
docs.text:
The workflow should be effortless:

Upload → Preview → Confirm → AI Processing → Results → Done

The user should understand exactly what the system is doing at every step.

docs.title: In Scope
docs.text:
• CSV Upload
  • CSV Preview
  • AI Field Mapping
  • CRM Extraction
  • Record Validation
  • Skip Invalid Records
  • Statistics
  • Responsive UI

docs.title: Out of Scope (Current Version)
docs.text:
• Authentication
  • Multi-user support
  • Persistent database
  • Import history
  • Team collaboration
  • Scheduling imports
  • CRM write-back APIs

These can be added in future versions.

docs.title: Persona 1 — Sales Executive
docs.text:
Uploads customer spreadsheets.

Needs:

  • Fast imports
  • Minimal manual work
  • High accuracy

docs.title: Persona 2 — Marketing Agency
docs.text:
Receives leads from multiple platforms.

Needs:

  • Automatic field mapping
  • Batch imports
  • Consistent CRM formatting

docs.title: Persona 3 — Operations Team
docs.text:
Receives data from many departments.

Needs:

  • Reliable imports
  • Validation
  • Error visibility

docs.title: Upload Module
docs.text:
Responsibilities:

  • Accept CSV
  • Validate file type
  • Validate size
  • Read metadata

Output: Raw File

docs.title: Preview Module
docs.text:
Responsibilities:

  • Parse CSV
  • Display rows
  • Display headers
  • Allow user verification

No AI processing occurs here.

docs.title: Import Module
docs.text:
Responsibilities:

  • Send confirmation
  • Upload parsed records
  • Track progress

docs.title: AI Engine
docs.text:
Responsibilities:

  • Understand column semantics
  • Extract CRM schema
  • Ignore irrelevant information
  • Handle ambiguous naming

docs.title: Result Module
docs.text:
Responsibilities — display:

  • Imported records
  • Skipped records
  • Summary
  • Statistics

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• Preview should render quickly for typical CSV sizes.
  • Batch processing should avoid excessively large AI requests.
  • Memory usage should remain predictable by streaming large files where appropriate.

docs.title: Scalability
docs.text:
Architecture should support growth from

100 rows → 10,000 rows → 100,000 rows

without requiring major architectural changes.

docs.title: Reliability
docs.text:
The system should never crash because:

  • AI failed
  • One batch failed
  • One row was malformed
  • One record contained invalid data

Graceful degradation is preferred over total failure.

docs.title: Maintainability
docs.text:
Every module should own one responsibility.

  • No business logic should leak into UI.
  • No AI prompt should be scattered across multiple files.

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
Protect against:

  • Malicious CSV uploads
  • Oversized files
  • Prompt injection through CSV content
  • Formula injection if exporting results later
  • Invalid MIME types

docs.title: Separation of Concerns
docs.text:
Each layer performs exactly one job:

CSV Parser ≠ AI Engine ≠ Validation ≠ Statistics

docs.title: AI as an Assistant
docs.text:
AI should never control the application. Instead:

Application → AI → Application validates AI → Application decides

The application remains the source of truth.

docs.title: Deterministic Before Probabilistic
docs.text:
Use deterministic logic wherever possible before invoking the LLM.

Examples:

  • Email detection
  • Phone normalization
  • Date parsing
  • Empty row detection

Reserve AI for semantic understanding and ambiguous mappings.

docs.title: Fail Gracefully
docs.text: If one batch fails, continue the remaining batches instead of aborting the entire import.

docs.title: 10. High-Level Architecture
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    U[User] --> FE[Next.js Frontend]
    FE --> API[Express REST API]
    API --> CSV[CSV Processing]
    API --> AI[AI Processing]
    CSV --> NORM[Normalization]
    AI --> PROMPT[Prompt Engine]
    NORM --> VAL[Validation Engine]
    PROMPT --> VAL
    VAL --> STATS[Statistics Engine]
    STATS --> RESP[JSON Response]
    RESP --> UI[Frontend UI]

docs.title: 11. Request Lifecycle
docs.text:
A single import request flows through the following stages:

flowchart TD
    A[Upload File] --> B[Validate File]
    B --> C[Parse CSV]
    C --> D[Generate Preview]
    D --> E[User Confirmation]
    E --> F[Normalize Data]
    F --> G[Create Batches]
    G --> H[AI Extraction]
    H --> I[Validate AI Output]
    I --> J[Apply Business Rules]
    J --> K[Generate Statistics]
    K --> L[Return Results]
    L --> M[Display Summary]

Each stage has clearly defined inputs and outputs, making the pipeline easier to test and evolve. The pipeline philosophy behind this lifecycle is developed in depth in Chapter 4 — The Pipeline Architecture Mindset.

docs.title: 12. Major System Components
docs.text: Component Responsibility Upload Module Accept and validate files CSV Parser Read CSV into structured records Normalization Engine Clean and standardize raw values Batch Manager Split work into manageable AI requests Prompt Engine Build structured prompts for the LLM AI Client Communicate with the selected model Validation Engine Verify schema and business rules CRM Mapper Produce canonical CRM records Statistics Engine Compute import metrics Result Formatter Shape the final API response

docs.title: 13. Success Metrics
docs.text: The project should be evaluated on more than "it works."

docs.title: Functional Success
docs.text:
• Correct field extraction
  • Correct skipped record detection
  • Valid CRM output

docs.title: Engineering Success
docs.text:
• Modular codebase
  • Clear architecture
  • Testability
  • Error handling
  • Documentation

docs.title: User Experience Success
docs.text:
• Simple workflow
  • Fast feedback
  • Clear progress
  • Helpful error messages
  • Actionable import summary

docs.title: 14. Future Evolution
docs.text:
The architecture is intentionally designed to grow into a generalized AI Data Ingestion Platform.

Potential future capabilities include:

  • Excel (.xlsx) ingestion
  • PDF table extraction
  • OCR from scanned documents
  • Direct CRM integrations
  • Webhook-based imports
  • Human-in-the-loop validation
  • Import history and auditing
  • Multi-tenant workspaces
  • Fine-tuned domain-specific extraction models

Because responsibilities are separated into independent modules, these features can be introduced incrementally without redesigning the core pipeline. See Chapter 20 — Future Evolution & Platform Vision.

Design Rationale: One architectural improvement over the original assignment framing: instead of thinking in terms of Frontend ↔ Backend ↔ AI, the system is designed as a processing pipeline where each stage has a well-defined contract. That makes the system easier to test, easier to debug, and much easier to extend — see Chapter 4 — The Pipeline Architecture Mindset. This architecture is then translated into a production-grade frontend in Chapter 6 — Frontend Architecture.

docs.title: Implementation Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] Task 5.1 — Product vision. Document the product vision and long-term direction for the intelligent data-ingestion platform.
  • [ ] Task 5.2 — Problem and personas. Capture the business problem statement and the target user personas with their needs.
  • [ ] Task 5.3 — Requirements specification. Define the functional requirements (per module) and non-functional requirements (performance, scalability, reliability, maintainability, security).
  • [ ] Task 5.4 — Design principles. Establish the design principles: separation of concerns, AI as an assistant, deterministic before probabilistic, and graceful failure.
  • [ ] Task 5.5 — High-level architecture. Produce the high-level system architecture spanning frontend, API, CSV/AI processing, validation, and statistics.
  • [ ] Task 5.6 — Request lifecycle. Define the end-to-end request lifecycle from upload through result display, with explicit inputs and outputs per stage.
  • [ ] Task 5.7 — Component inventory. Enumerate the core system modules and assign each a single responsibility.
  • [ ] Task 5.8 — Success metrics and roadmap. Define functional, engineering, and UX success metrics plus the future-evolution roadmap.

docs.title: Related Chapters
docs.text:
• Chapter 1 — Assignment Specification — the original brief this architecture is designed to exceed
  • Chapter 4 — The Pipeline Architecture Mindset — the pipeline philosophy underlying the request lifecycle
  • Chapter 6 — Frontend Architecture — translates this architecture into the frontend blueprint
  • Chapter 7 — Backend Architecture — implements the backend side of this architecture
  • Chapter 20 — Future Evolution & Platform Vision — expands the future-evolution roadmap

docs.title: Chapter 6 — Frontend Architecture
docs.text:
Goal: Design a frontend that is scalable, maintainable, highly responsive, and acts as the orchestration layer for the AI Data Ingestion Engine.

The frontend is not "just a UI." In this system it acts as the orchestrator of the user workflow: it validates user actions, provides visibility into each processing stage, and communicates with the backend through well-defined API contracts rather than embedding business logic.

docs.title: 1. Frontend Philosophy
docs.text:
A typical quick solution builds the frontend as a handful of route-level pages:

/pages
  Upload.tsx
  Preview.tsx
  Result.tsx

Everything becomes mixed together — UI, API calls, business logic, state, and validation — and eventually you get a 700-line React component.

Instead, we build it like a SaaS application. The frontend has one responsibility:

Present information, collect user interactions, and orchestrate the workflow.

It should never:

  • understand CRM mapping
  • validate AI output
  • normalize phone numbers
  • process business rules

Those belong to backend services (see Chapter 7 — Backend Architecture).

docs.title: 2. Frontend Responsibilities
docs.text:
The frontend should only do these things:

Upload File → Validate Basic Constraints → Preview CSV → Ask User Confirmation
→ Start Import → Track Progress → Display Results → Allow Export

No AI logic. No CRM logic. No parsing logic beyond preview.

docs.title: 3. User Journey
docs.text:
The user experience, end to end:

Landing Page → Upload CSV → CSV Preview → File Statistics → Confirm Import
→ Import Progress → Result Dashboard → Download Result

Each page is actually a state, not a route.

docs.title: 4. Single Page Application
docs.text:
Instead of separate routes (/upload, /preview, /import, /result), we build a single route (/) that renders different UI states.

Reason: the user never leaves the workflow. No page reload, no lost state — a much smoother UX.

docs.title: 5. Application State Machine
docs.text:
Think of the frontend as a finite state machine with six states:

  • Idle
  • Uploading
  • Preview Ready
  • Importing
  • Completed
  • Error

Only one state exists at a time. An example transition sequence:

flowchart TD
    A[Idle] -->|Upload success| B[Preview Ready]
    B -->|User clicks Confirm| C[Importing]
    C -->|Finished| D[Completed]

This avoids dozens of boolean flags like

isUploading, isLoading, isPreview, isImporting, isComplete, showTable, showResult, ...

Instead, a single currentState value controls the UI.

docs.title: 6. Folder Structure
docs.text:
Instead of organizing by routes, organize by features:

src/
  app/
  components/
    upload/
    preview/
    import/
    result/
    shared/
  hooks/
  services/
  stores/
  lib/
  types/
  utils/
  constants/
  styles/

Everything has one responsibility.

docs.title: 7. Component Hierarchy
docs.text:
App
│
├── Navbar
├── WorkflowStepper
├── UploadSection
├── PreviewSection
├── ImportProgress
├── ResultDashboard
└── Footer

Each section is independent.

docs.title: 8. Upload Module
docs.text:
Responsibilities:

  • Drag & Drop
  • File Picker
  • Validation
  • Display File Info

Nothing else. Output: Selected File.

docs.title: 9. Preview Module
docs.text:
Responsibilities — display:

  • Columns
  • Rows
  • CSV Preview
  • Scrollable Table

Show statistics:

  • rows
  • columns
  • file size

No AI. No import.

docs.title: 10. Statistics Panel
docs.text:
Before importing, show useful information:

Rows
Columns
File Size
Detected Delimiter
Estimated Batches
Estimated Processing Time
Estimated AI Calls

Surfacing this information builds user trust through transparency.

docs.title: 11. Import Module
docs.text:
Very small responsibility:

User clicks Confirm → POST → Receive Job → Track Progress

No processing happens here.

docs.title: 12. Progress UI
docs.text:
Instead of a bare spinner (Loading...), build stage-aware progress:

Uploading
██████████
Complete

Processing
█████░░░░░
Batch 12 / 30

Imported
321

Skipped
18

This is the level of feedback users expect from an enterprise product.

docs.title: 13. Result Dashboard
docs.text:
Instead of only a table, build a dashboard of summary metrics:

Imported          420
Skipped           17
Success           96%
Time              14 sec
Batches           18
Detected Fields   15

Below the metrics, render two tables:

  • Imported Records Table
  • Skipped Records Table

docs.title: 14. Reusable Components
docs.text:
Never repeat UI. Create reusable components, then compose screens from them:

  • Button
  • Card
  • Badge
  • Alert
  • Table
  • Modal
  • Progress Bar
  • Stat Card
  • Upload Zone
  • Loading Overlay
  • Empty State
  • Error State

docs.title: 15. Table Component
docs.text:
Tables are a core requirement of the product, so build one powerful, future-proof component with these capabilities:

  • Sticky Header
  • Horizontal Scroll
  • Vertical Scroll
  • Responsive
  • Virtualization
  • Column Resize (optional)
  • Sorting

docs.title: 16. State Management
docs.text: Avoid prop drilling, and think about the different kinds of state separately.

docs.title: UI State
docs.text: Current Step, Dark Mode, Sidebar, Dialogs

docs.title: Upload State
docs.text: Selected File, File Size, File Name, Errors

docs.title: Preview State
docs.text: Headers, Rows, Statistics

docs.title: Import State
docs.text: Progress, Current Batch, Remaining, Completed

docs.title: Result State
docs.text:
Imported, Skipped, Summary

Keep them separate instead of creating one massive state object.

docs.title: 17. API Layer
docs.text:
Never call fetch inside components. Instead:

Component → Service → API → Backend

Benefits: easy to mock, easy to test, easy to replace.

docs.title: 18. Custom Hooks
docs.text:
Think in terms of behaviors:

  • Upload Logic
  • Preview Logic
  • Import Logic
  • Progress Logic

Components stay clean.

docs.title: 19. Error Handling
docs.text:
Errors are part of UX. Differentiate between error classes:

  • Upload Error
  • Preview Error
  • Import Error
  • Network Error
  • Server Error
  • AI Error

Each deserves different messaging.

docs.title: 20. Loading Strategy
docs.text: Every async action should have a skeleton or a progress indicator. Never blank screens.

docs.title: 21. Accessibility
docs.text:
Support:

  • Keyboard Upload
  • ARIA Labels
  • Focus Management
  • Screen Readers
  • Large Click Targets
  • High Contrast

These go beyond the minimum requirements but are standard in production applications.

docs.title: 22. Responsive Design
docs.text:
Desktop layout:

Statistics
Table
Summary

Mobile layout:

Statistics → Cards → Scrollable Table

Never shrink the table — allow horizontal scrolling instead.

docs.title: 23. Theme System
docs.text: Implement three modes: Light, Dark, and System. Not strictly required, but expected of a polished product.

docs.title: 24. Frontend Data Flow
docs.text:
The entire frontend flows in one direction:

User → Upload Component → Upload State → Preview Component → Confirm
→ Import Service → Progress Store → Result Component → Dashboard

Everything flows in one direction. No circular dependencies.

docs.title: 25. Communication with Backend
docs.text:
The frontend should treat the backend as a contract-driven service. A clean sequence looks like:

sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant F as Frontend
    participant B as Backend
    U->>F: Upload CSV
    F->>B: POST /preview
    B-->>F: Preview Data
    U->>F: Confirms import
    F->>B: POST /import
    B-->>F: Import Started
    B-->>F: Progress Updates
    B-->>F: Final Result

Whether progress is implemented with polling or server-sent events can be decided later in the backend architecture (Chapter 7 — Backend Architecture), but the frontend should be designed so the underlying transport can change without affecting UI components.

docs.title: 26. Frontend Design Principles
docs.text:
Every design decision in this chapter follows these principles:

  • Single Responsibility: Each component has one purpose.
  • Composition over Monoliths: Build complex screens from small reusable pieces.
  • Feature-Based Organization: Group code by business capability, not file type.
  • Unidirectional Data Flow: Data moves predictably from user actions to UI updates.
  • Backend as Source of Truth: Business rules and AI logic remain on the server.
  • Reusable Contracts: Components consume typed data models instead of raw API responses.

docs.title: 27. Final Frontend Architecture
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    U[User] --> APP["App (Workflow Shell)"]
    APP --> UP[Upload Module]
    APP --> PV[Preview Module]
    APP --> RS[Result Module]
    UP --> SM[State Management]
    PV --> SM
    RS --> SM
    SM --> SVC[Service Layer]
    SVC --> HTTP[HTTP API Client]
    HTTP --> BE[Backend API]

The frontend never performs CRM extraction, normalization, or AI reasoning. Its role is to provide an intuitive workflow, maintain application state, and render the results of the backend pipeline (see Chapter 4 — The Pipeline Architecture Mindset).

docs.title: Implementation Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] Task 6.1 — Workflow state machine. Implement the single-page workflow as a finite state machine (Idle, Uploading, Preview Ready, Importing, Completed, Error) driven by a single currentState.
  • [ ] Task 6.2 — Feature-based folder structure. Set up the src/ structure organized by feature (upload, preview, import, result, shared) with hooks, services, stores, lib, types, utils, constants, and styles.
  • [ ] Task 6.3 — Component hierarchy. Build the App shell with Navbar, WorkflowStepper, UploadSection, PreviewSection, ImportProgress, ResultDashboard, and Footer as independent sections.
  • [ ] Task 6.4 — State management. Implement separated UI, upload, preview, import, and result state stores instead of one massive state object.
  • [ ] Task 6.5 — API service layer. Create a service layer and HTTP API client so no component calls fetch directly, with custom hooks for upload, preview, import, and progress behaviors.
  • [ ] Task 6.6 — Reusable UI kit. Build the reusable component library (Button, Card, Badge, Alert, Table, Modal, Progress Bar, Stat Card, Upload Zone, Loading Overlay, Empty State, Error State).
  • [ ] Task 6.7 — Responsive table and dashboard. Implement the powerful table component (sticky header, scrolling, virtualization, sorting) and the result dashboard with summary metrics plus imported/skipped tables.
  • [ ] Task 6.8 — Error, loading, and accessibility strategy. Differentiate error classes with distinct messaging, add skeleton/progress states for every async action, and implement accessibility support (ARIA, keyboard, focus management, contrast).
  • [ ] Task 6.9 — Backend interaction model. Implement the preview/import/progress contract so the progress transport (polling or server-sent events) can change without affecting UI components.

docs.title: Related Chapters
docs.text:
• Chapter 4 — The Pipeline Architecture Mindset — the pipeline whose stages this frontend visualizes and orchestrates
  • Chapter 5 — Product Thinking & System Architecture — the system architecture and UX objectives this frontend implements
  • Chapter 7 — Backend Architecture — the contract-driven backend this frontend communicates with
  • Chapter 15 — Observability, Telemetry & Operational Intelligence — where import progress and statistics originate on the server side

docs.title: Chapter 7 — Backend Architecture
docs.text: Goal: Design a backend that behaves like a production AI ingestion platform rather than a simple Express application.

docs.title: 1. Backend Philosophy
docs.text:
A naive backend for this problem looks like this:

POST /upload → Read CSV → Call OpenAI → Return JSON

It works for small demos. But after a few features, everything ends up inside one service:

  • CSV parsing
  • Validation
  • Prompt building
  • AI calls
  • Business rules
  • Statistics
  • Response formatting

Eventually you have a 1000-line service file. That is exactly what this architecture avoids.

docs.title: Our Philosophy
docs.text:
The backend should be a pipeline orchestrator, not a collection of API endpoints.

Instead of thinking

Express → Controller → AI

think

Express → Pipeline → Independent Processing Stages → Response

Express is only the entry point. The actual business value lives inside the pipeline. (See Chapter 4 — The Pipeline Architecture Mindset for the reasoning behind this model.)

docs.title: 2. Architectural Style
docs.text:
We follow a layered architecture inspired by Clean Architecture.

flowchart TD
    C[Client] --> HC[HTTP Controller]
    HC --> AL[Application Layer]
    AL --> PP[Processing Pipeline]
    PP --> D[Domain]
    PP --> I[Infrastructure]
    PP --> AI[AI]
    I --> EXT[External APIs]

Notice: the pipeline doesn't know anything about Express. That makes it reusable.

docs.title: Controllers
docs.text:
Responsible only for:

  • reading the request
  • validating request format
  • invoking the pipeline
  • returning the response

Controllers never contain business logic.

docs.title: Application Layer
docs.text:
Coordinates modules. Think of it as

The project manager.

It decides which stage executes next.

docs.title: Domain Layer
docs.text:
Contains business rules. Example:

Skip record IF Email == null AND Phone == null

This logic belongs here.

docs.title: Infrastructure Layer
docs.text:
Everything external:

  • OpenAI
  • File System
  • Logger
  • Configuration
  • HTTP Client

Infrastructure can change without affecting the business rules.

docs.title: 4. Backend Folder Structure
docs.text:
Instead of the traditional

controllers/
models/
routes/

we organize around responsibilities:

src/
    api/
        controllers/
        routes/
        middleware/
    pipeline/
        stages/
    domain/
        entities/
        rules/
        services/
    infrastructure/
        ai/
        csv/
        logger/
        config/
    shared/
        errors/
        types/
        constants/
        utils/
    app.ts
    server.ts

This is much easier to scale.

docs.title: 5. Pipeline Directory
docs.text:
This becomes the heart of the application.

pipeline/
    validate-upload/
    parse-csv/
    normalize/
    batching/
    ai-extraction/
    validation/
    business-rules/
    statistics/
    formatter/

Every folder: one responsibility.

docs.title: 6. Request Lifecycle
docs.text:
Tracing one request:

POST
  → Controller
  → Create Pipeline Context
  → Run Stage 1
  → Run Stage 2
  → Run Stage 3
  → Run Stage 4
  → Run Stage 5
  → Return Result

Notice: the controller doesn't know what happens inside.

docs.title: 7. Pipeline Context
docs.text:
Instead of passing twenty variables between stages, create one context object that accumulates state:

Pipeline Context
  ├── Current File
  ├── Rows
  ├── Normalized Rows
  ├── Batches
  ├── Results
  ├── Statistics
  └── Errors

Every stage updates the context. This makes the pipeline very clean.

docs.title: 8. Pipeline Contract
docs.text:
Every stage follows the same interface. Conceptually:

Input → Process → Output

Every stage should be replaceable. For example, the current parser is a CSV Parser; in the future it could be an Excel Parser — and no other code changes.

docs.title: 9. Processing Stages
docs.text:
The backend pipeline becomes:

flowchart TD
    A[Validate Upload] --> B[Parse CSV]
    B --> C[Normalize Data]
    C --> D[Create Batches]
    D --> E[AI Extraction]
    E --> F[Validate AI Output]
    F --> G[Apply Business Rules]
    G --> H[Generate Statistics]
    H --> I[Format Response]

This is exactly the same pipeline defined in Chapter 5 — Product Thinking & System Architecture.

docs.title: 10. Controllers
docs.text:
Controllers should remain tiny. Think

Receive Request → Call Pipeline → Return Response

not

Receive → Parse CSV → Normalize → OpenAI → Validate → Statistics → Return

Controllers should be boring. That's a good thing.

docs.title: 11. Services
docs.text:
Each service owns one capability:

Service Responsibility CSV Service Reads CSV AI Service Communicates with the LLM Validation Service Checks extracted records Statistics Service Counts imported rows

Never mix responsibilities.

docs.title: 12. Domain Rules
docs.text:
This is where architectural maturity shows. Instead of writing

if (email == null && phone == null)

inside a controller, create a named Lead Validation Rules module in the domain layer. The rule becomes reusable: a future importer of a different format applies the same rules unchanged.

docs.title: 13. Infrastructure
docs.text:
Infrastructure contains:

  • OpenAI Client
  • HTTP Client
  • Logger
  • Configuration
  • Storage
  • CSV Reader

Infrastructure should never contain business logic.

docs.title: 14. Middleware
docs.text:
Middleware only handles cross-cutting concerns:

  • Request Logging
  • Error Handling
  • Rate Limiting
  • Request ID
  • File Upload
  • Security Headers

Never place CRM logic here.

docs.title: 15. Error Handling
docs.text:
Never throw random errors. Create a structured hierarchy:

ApplicationError
 ├── ValidationError
 ├── AIError
 ├── ParsingError
 ├── FileError
 ├── RateLimitError
 └── InternalError

Benefit: every error becomes predictable.

docs.title: 16. Logging
docs.text:
Every request should receive a Request ID. Example lifecycle:

Request Received
  → CSV Parsed
  → Rows: 1400
  → Normalized
  → Created 28 batches
  → AI Processing
  → Validation Complete
  → Statistics Generated
  → Response Sent

If a customer reports an issue, one Request ID tells the entire story. Observability is expanded in Chapter 15 — Observability, Telemetry & Operational Intelligence.

docs.title: 17. Configuration Management
docs.text:
Never scatter values across files. Configuration should own:

  • Max File Size
  • Batch Size
  • Retry Count
  • Timeout
  • AI Model
  • Temperature
  • API Keys

One source of truth.

docs.title: 18. Dependency Flow
docs.text:
Dependencies should only flow downward:

Controller → Application → Pipeline → Domain → Infrastructure

Never the opposite. For example, Infrastructure should never import a Controller. This keeps coupling low.

docs.title: 19. Why the Pipeline Is Powerful
docs.text:
Imagine tomorrow the company says

"Support Excel."

The current CSV Parser stage becomes an Excel Parser. Everything below stays exactly the same. Tomorrow: support PDF — replace the parser; the pipeline survives.

docs.title: 20. Backend Scalability
docs.text:
The current system is synchronous, but the architecture supports future evolution:

Request → Queue → Worker → Pipeline → Storage → Notification

We don't need queues today, but we shouldn't prevent adding them tomorrow. Concurrency and orchestration are covered in Chapter 14 — Execution Engine, Orchestration & Concurrency.

docs.title: 21. AI Isolation
docs.text:
One of the most important decisions: never allow business logic to directly call OpenAI. Instead:

Pipeline → AI Service → OpenAI Client

Tomorrow, replace OpenAI with Gemini: only the AI Client changes; everything else survives. This is called the Adapter Pattern. The AI layer itself is designed in Chapter 10 — AI Extraction Engine.

docs.title: 22. API Design
docs.text:
Keep APIs focused on user actions rather than internal implementation. For this system, three endpoints are sufficient:

Endpoint Purpose POST /preview Upload and preview the CSV without AI processing POST /import Execute the ingestion pipeline and return structured CRM data GET /health Health check for deployment and monitoring

Avoid exposing internal pipeline stages as separate APIs. They are implementation details.

docs.title: 23. Response Strategy
docs.text:
Every API response should follow a consistent envelope:

success
requestId
data
metadata
errors

Consistency simplifies frontend development and future integrations.

docs.title: 24. Backend Component Diagram
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    API[Express API] --> RD[Route Dispatcher]
    RD --> CT[Controller]
    CT --> PR[Pipeline Runner]
    PR --> UP[Upload]
    PR --> CP[CSV Parser]
    PR --> NM[Normalizer]
    PR --> BB[Batch Builder]
    PR --> AS[AI Service]
    AS --> VA[Validation]
    VA --> BR[Business Rules]
    BR --> ST[Statistics]
    ST --> RB[Response Builder]

Notice that every component has exactly one responsibility and communicates through well-defined interfaces.

docs.title: 25. Engineering Decisions
docs.text: Decision Reason Layered architecture Keeps responsibilities separated Pipeline-based processing Easier testing and extensibility Thin controllers Prevents business logic leakage Domain-driven rules Business logic remains reusable Infrastructure isolation External providers become replaceable Structured errors Predictable debugging and monitoring Centralized configuration Easier maintenance across environments Adapter for AI providers Swap OpenAI, Gemini, Claude with minimal changes

docs.title: 26. Backend Principles
docs.text:
The backend is designed around these rules:

  • Controllers orchestrate; they do not think.
  • Services solve one problem each.
  • The pipeline owns the workflow.
  • Domain rules define business behavior.
  • Infrastructure integrates with external systems.
  • Every processing stage has a clear contract.
  • External dependencies are replaceable.
  • Failures are isolated and recoverable.

docs.title: Implementation Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] Task 7.1 — Layered backend skeleton. Create the production-grade backend structure (api/, pipeline/, domain/, infrastructure/, shared/, app.ts, server.ts) with strictly downward dependency flow.
  • [ ] Task 7.2 — Layered responsibility model. Implement thin controllers, an application coordination layer, domain rules, and infrastructure adapters with no cross-layer leakage.
  • [ ] Task 7.3 — Pipeline execution model. Build a pipeline runner that executes stages sequentially over a shared Pipeline Context object, with each stage implementing a common Input → Process → Output contract.
  • [ ] Task 7.4 — Pipeline stage folders. Scaffold one directory per stage: validate-upload, parse-csv, normalize, batching, ai-extraction, validation, business-rules, statistics, formatter.
  • [ ] Task 7.5 — Processing stages. Implement the nine-stage flow: Validate Upload → Parse CSV → Normalize Data → Create Batches → AI Extraction → Validate AI Output → Apply Business Rules → Generate Statistics → Format Response.
  • [ ] Task 7.6 — Error hierarchy. Define ApplicationError with ValidationError, AIError, ParsingError, FileError, RateLimitError, and InternalError subclasses, plus centralized error-handling middleware.
  • [ ] Task 7.7 — Logging strategy. Assign a Request ID to every request and log each pipeline milestone (parsed rows, batch counts, AI processing, validation, statistics, response) under that ID.
  • [ ] Task 7.8 — Configuration management. Centralize max file size, batch size, retry count, timeout, AI model, temperature, and API keys into a single configuration module.
  • [ ] Task 7.9 — AI abstraction layer. Isolate all LLM access behind an AI Service and provider adapter so OpenAI can be swapped for Gemini or Claude without touching business logic.
  • [ ] Task 7.10 — API surface. Expose exactly three endpoints — POST /preview, POST /import, GET /health — with a consistent response envelope (success, requestId, data, metadata, errors).
  • [ ] Task 7.11 — Scalability path. Keep the pipeline decoupled from Express so a future Request → Queue → Worker → Pipeline → Storage → Notification model can be added without restructuring.

docs.title: Related Chapters
docs.text:
• Chapter 4 — The Pipeline Architecture Mindset — the conceptual foundation for treating the backend as a pipeline orchestrator
  • Chapter 5 — Product Thinking & System Architecture — defines the end-to-end pipeline that this backend implements
  • Chapter 8 — CSV Processing Engine — the first major processing stage invoked by this backend pipeline
  • Chapter 14 — Execution Engine, Orchestration & Concurrency — how the pipeline runner executes stages and batches at scale
  • Chapter 15 — Observability, Telemetry & Operational Intelligence — expands the Request ID logging strategy into full telemetry

docs.title: Chapter 8 — CSV Processing Engine
docs.text:
Goal: Build a production-grade CSV Processing Engine that can reliably ingest messy, real-world CSV files and produce a clean, structured dataset for downstream AI processing.

Core Principle: Never let the AI solve problems that deterministic code can solve first.

docs.title: 1. Why This Module Is Critical
docs.text:
A naive design sends the file straight to the model:

CSV → AI

This is a mistake. If the CSV contains malformed rows, duplicate headers, invalid encodings, inconsistent delimiters, or noisy data, the AI has to work harder, costs more, and becomes less reliable.

Instead, we build a Data Preparation Engine before AI:

flowchart LR
    CSV[CSV] --> V[Validation]
    V --> P[Parsing]
    P --> C[Cleaning]
    C --> N[Normalization]
    N --> M[Metadata]
    M --> AI[AI]

The AI should receive structured, predictable input, not raw spreadsheets.

docs.title: 2. Responsibilities
docs.text:
The CSV Processing Engine owns only file ingestion and structural understanding.

It is not responsible for:

  • CRM mapping
  • AI extraction
  • Business rules
  • Validation of CRM fields

It is responsible for:

  • Reading the file
  • Understanding its structure
  • Producing structured records
  • Producing metadata
  • Detecting parsing issues

docs.title: 3. Processing Pipeline
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    U[Upload] --> FV[File Validation]
    FV --> ED[Encoding Detection]
    ED --> DD[Delimiter Detection]
    DD --> CP[CSV Parsing]
    CP --> HA[Header Analysis]
    HA --> RV[Row Validation]
    RV --> ME[Metadata Extraction]
    ME --> SR[Structured Records]

Each stage performs one responsibility.

docs.title: 4. Stage 1 — File Validation
docs.text:
Before opening the file, validate:

  • Extension
  • MIME type
  • File size
  • Empty file
  • Corrupted upload

Example of an accepted file:

marketing.csv
✓ CSV
✓ 2.4 MB
✓ Not Empty
✓ Accepted

Reject immediately if the upload is:

.exe
.zip
.pdf
0 bytes
> configured limit

Never waste resources parsing an invalid file.

docs.title: 5. Stage 2 — Encoding Detection
docs.text:
Real-world CSVs use different encodings:

UTF-8
UTF-8 BOM
UTF-16
ISO-8859-1
Windows-1252

If the encoding is wrong, names become

JosÃ©
MÃ¼ller
FranÃ§ois

instead of

José
Müller
François

Encoding detection happens before parsing.

docs.title: 6. Stage 3 — Delimiter Detection
docs.text:
Not every CSV uses commas. Common delimiters:

,
;
|
\t

Examples of non-comma files:

Name;Email;Phone

Name|Email|Phone

The engine should inspect the first few lines and infer the delimiter instead of assuming ,.

docs.title: 7. Stage 4 — Streaming Parser
docs.text:
Never read the whole file into memory if you don't have to.

Bad:

Read Entire File → Memory

Good:

Read Chunk → Parse → Emit Row → Next Chunk

Benefits:

  • lower memory usage
  • scalable to large files
  • faster first preview
  • future-ready for very large imports

docs.title: 8. Stage 5 — Header Detection
docs.text:
Headers are surprisingly messy. The same concept appears as:

Email
email
EMAIL
Email Address
Primary Email
Mail ID
Customer Email

At this stage we do not map them. We only identify:

  • header row
  • number of columns
  • duplicate names
  • empty names

Output example:

Detected Headers: 17
Duplicate Headers: 1
Unnamed Columns: 2

docs.title: 9. Header Canonicalization
docs.text:
Normalize header formatting without changing meaning:

Original Canonical Email Address email address Customer_Name customer name Lead-Owner lead owner

This improves downstream semantic matching (see Chapter 12 — Semantic Intelligence & Prompt Orchestration).

docs.title: 10. Row Parsing
docs.text:
Each row becomes:

{
  "rowNumber": 52,
  "cells": {
      "Customer Name": "John",
      "Mail": "john@gmail.com",
      "Phone": "9876543210"
  }
}

Important: the parser should preserve the original values. No AI. No normalization.

docs.title: 11. Malformed Row Recovery
docs.text:
Real CSVs are messy. Example:

John,"Hello
World",9876543210

Quoted fields may contain commas or line breaks. A robust CSV parser should recover these correctly instead of splitting rows incorrectly.

If a row cannot be recovered:

  • record the parsing error
  • skip the row
  • continue processing

Never fail the entire import because of one malformed record.

docs.title: 12. Empty Row Detection
docs.text:
Rows like

,,,,,

or

"","",""

provide no value. Detect them early and mark them:

Skipped
Reason: Empty Row

docs.title: 13. Duplicate Header Handling
docs.text:
Example:

Email
Email
Phone

This creates ambiguity.

Strategy: keep both columns internally with unique identifiers while preserving the original names for diagnostics:

Email
Email (2)

The AI can later see both values if needed.

docs.title: 14. Missing Header Strategy
docs.text:
Sometimes exports look like

John
john@gmail.com
9876543210

No headers. The engine should detect this and reject the import with a clear explanation rather than guessing.

docs.title: 15. Metadata Extraction
docs.text:
The parser should generate metadata alongside records. Example:

Field Value Rows 1450 Columns 18 Delimiter Comma Encoding UTF-8 Header Count 18 Blank Rows 4

This metadata powers the preview UI and logging.

docs.title: 16. Column Profiling
docs.text:
This is an important feature that is easy to overlook. Instead of only reading values, profile every column.

Example — column Phone:

98% Numeric
Average Length: 10
Unique Values: 1450
Missing: 2%

Example — column Email:

Email Pattern: 96%
Missing: 4%

This information becomes extremely valuable for AI and debugging.

docs.title: 17. Data Type Inference
docs.text:
Without AI, infer likely data types:

Value Inferred Type 2026-05-14 Date 9876543210 Phone Candidate john@gmail.com Email Candidate Mumbai Text

This is not CRM mapping. It is structural understanding.

docs.title: 18. Parsing Statistics
docs.text:
Generate:

Rows Parsed
Rows Failed
Rows Empty
Headers
Duplicate Headers
Average Columns
Missing Cells
Maximum Row Width

The frontend can visualize this before import.

docs.title: 19. Error Recovery
docs.text:
The parser should isolate failures. Instead of

Import Failed

return

Rows Parsed: 1450
Failed Rows: 2
Reason: Malformed Quotes

This is much more useful.

docs.title: 20. Output Contract
docs.text:
The parser should return a well-defined object. Conceptually:

File Metadata
+
Column Metadata
+
Parsed Records
+
Parsing Diagnostics

Downstream stages should never need to reopen or reinterpret the original CSV.

docs.title: 21. Why This Improves AI
docs.text:
Instead of sending an unknown CSV, the AI receives:

  • clean rows
  • consistent headers
  • normalized structure
  • metadata
  • row identifiers

That reduces ambiguity and token waste.

docs.title: 22. Future-Proof Design
docs.text:
Suppose tomorrow we add Excel support. Only the CSV Parser is replaced. The output contract remains identical:

Metadata + Rows + Diagnostics

Every downstream module continues to work unchanged. This is why contracts matter more than implementations.

docs.title: 23. Internal Component Architecture
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    E[CSV Processing Engine] --> FV[File Validation]
    E --> SA[Structure Analyzer]
    E --> DC[Diagnostics Collector]
    FV --> ED[Encoding Detection]
    SA --> DD[Delimiter Detection]
    DC --> PE[Parsing Errors]
    ED --> SP[Streaming Parser]
    DD --> SP
    SP --> HP[Header Processor]
    HP --> RP[Row Processor]
    RP --> MG[Metadata Generator]
    MG --> SD[Structured Dataset]

Each component has a single responsibility and contributes to the final structured dataset.

docs.title: 24. Engineering Decisions
docs.text: Decision Why Streaming parser Handles large files efficiently Automatic delimiter detection Supports diverse CSV exports Encoding detection Prevents corrupted text Header canonicalization Improves downstream semantic matching Column profiling Gives AI richer context Structured diagnostics Easier debugging and UX Contract-based output Enables parser replacement without pipeline changes

docs.title: 25. Architectural Improvement Over the Assignment
docs.text:
The original assignment says:

"Parse CSV."

Our architecture expands that into a dedicated ingestion subsystem that understands the structure and quality of the dataset before any AI is involved. This improves reliability, lowers LLM costs, simplifies debugging, and creates a reusable foundation for future input formats such as Excel, JSON, or APIs. (See Chapter 1 — Assignment Specification for the original brief.)

docs.title: Implementation Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] Task 8.1 — File validation. Validate extension, MIME type, file size, empty files, and corrupted uploads before any parsing begins.
  • [ ] Task 8.2 — Encoding detection. Detect UTF-8, UTF-8 BOM, UTF-16, ISO-8859-1, and Windows-1252 encodings before parsing to prevent corrupted text.
  • [ ] Task 8.3 — Delimiter detection. Inspect the first lines of the file to infer ,, ;, |, or \t instead of assuming commas.
  • [ ] Task 8.4 — Streaming parser. Parse the file chunk-by-chunk, emitting rows incrementally for low memory usage and large-file scalability.
  • [ ] Task 8.5 — Header analysis. Identify the header row, column count, duplicate names, and empty names without performing any CRM mapping.
  • [ ] Task 8.6 — Header canonicalization. Normalize header formatting (case, underscores, hyphens) without changing meaning to aid semantic matching.
  • [ ] Task 8.7 — Row parsing. Emit each row as a { rowNumber, cells } record that preserves original values with no normalization.
  • [ ] Task 8.8 — Malformed row recovery. Recover quoted fields containing commas or line breaks; record and skip unrecoverable rows without failing the import.
  • [ ] Task 8.9 — Empty row detection. Detect and mark valueless rows (,,,,, or "","","") as skipped with reason "Empty Row".
  • [ ] Task 8.10 — Duplicate header handling. Keep duplicate columns internally with unique identifiers (e.g., Email, Email (2)) while preserving original names for diagnostics.
  • [ ] Task 8.11 — Missing header rejection. Detect headerless files and reject the import with a clear explanation rather than guessing.
  • [ ] Task 8.12 — Metadata extraction. Generate file-level metadata (rows, columns, delimiter, encoding, header count, blank rows) to power the preview UI and logging.
  • [ ] Task 8.13 — Column profiling and type inference. Profile every column (pattern match rate, average length, unique values, missing rate) and infer structural types (date, phone candidate, email candidate, text) without AI.
  • [ ] Task 8.14 — Diagnostics and output contract. Return parsing statistics and a stable contract of File Metadata + Column Metadata + Parsed Records + Parsing Diagnostics so downstream stages never reopen the original CSV.

docs.text:

  • Chapter 9 — Data Normalization Engine — the next stage, which cleans and standardizes the parsed values
  • Chapter 10 — AI Extraction Engine — the consumer of this engine's structured, metadata-rich output
  • Chapter 12 — Semantic Intelligence & Prompt Orchestration — builds on header canonicalization and column profiling for semantic mapping
  • Chapter 13 — Validation, Business Rules & Trust Engine — CRM-field validation, which is explicitly out of scope for this engine

docs.title: Chapter 9 — Data Normalization Engine
docs.text:
Goal: Transform messy, inconsistent, real-world CSV values into a clean, standardized dataset before any AI processing.

Core Principle: Every piece of deterministic cleaning done before the LLM reduces cost, improves accuracy, and lowers hallucinations.

docs.title: 1. Why a Normalization Engine Exists
docs.text:
This is one of the biggest differences between a demo project and a production system.

A naive implementation looks like this:

CSV → OpenAI → CRM JSON

AIDE instead inserts deterministic stages around the AI:

flowchart LR
    A[CSV] --> B[Parsing]
    B --> C[Normalization]
    C --> D[AI]
    D --> E[Validation]

Why? Because AI should understand meaning, not fix formatting mistakes.

docs.title: 2. What is Data Normalization?
docs.text:
Normalization means converting different representations of the same information into one consistent representation.

Example — input:

John
 john
JOHN
John
John

Output:

John

Another example — input:

+91 98765-43210
9876543210
(+91)9876543210
91-9876543210

Output:

+919876543210

The meaning never changes. Only the representation changes.

docs.title: 3. Philosophy
docs.text:
Think of this engine as a Data Refinery: raw oil enters the refinery and petrol comes out. Similarly:

Messy Data → Normalization → Clean Data

Only then does the data reach the AI.

docs.title: 4. Pipeline
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[Parsed Records] --> B[Whitespace Cleanup]
    B --> C[Unicode Cleanup]
    C --> D[Null Detection]
    D --> E[Phone Normalization]
    E --> F[Email Normalization]
    F --> G[Date Normalization]
    G --> H[Location Cleanup]
    H --> I[Duplicate Detection]
    I --> J[Canonical Records]

docs.title: 5. Responsibilities
docs.text:
This engine owns:

  • Cleaning
  • Formatting
  • Standardization
  • Type refinement

It never:

  • infers CRM fields
  • maps semantics
  • calls AI

Semantic mapping is the job of the AI Extraction Engine — see Chapter 10 — AI Extraction Engine.

docs.title: 6. Stage 1 — Whitespace Cleanup
docs.text:
Real CSVs contain values like:

" John "
"   Mumbai"
"john@gmail.com   "

Normalize to:

John
Mumbai
john@gmail.com

Also collapse repeated spaces:

John      Doe  →  John Doe

docs.title: 7. Stage 2 — Empty Value Detection
docs.text:
Many exports represent empty values differently:

NULL
null
N/A
NA
-
--
Unknown
Empty String
Whitespace

All of these normalize to a single canonical null. Now every downstream stage understands missing values consistently.

docs.title: 8. Stage 3 — Unicode Normalization
docs.text:
